These stones, enumerated above, are auspicious and hence
ought not to be broken, since they are always tenanted by Yaksas
and Nagas. Those kings, in whose kingdoms such stones or rocks
are found, will never experience drought.
[The metre ol thii> and the following two verses is
or STOTfif.]

Exploration of Water Springs L1V 623
Next the author mentions some methods of breaking stones.
Sf? tt?r 4ffr fsi^i afrfl ^ t
ggTEffifftTT
When a stone does not break ever when it is hammered, a fire
should be burnt on it with the logs of Palasa (Butea frondosa) and
Tinduka (a sort of ebony) trees until the stone assumes the colour
of the fire i.e. red, and then it should be sprinkled with lime water.
Then it will break easily.
[According to Utpala gqr means 5^ or milk, which is patently
wrong.
